does: This paper demonstrates that reward models trained on human preference data in one language can be directly applied to align language models in other languages without retraining, a zero-shot cross-lingual transfer approach. The authors evaluate this method on summarization and open-ended dialogue tasks across multiple language pairs, measuring alignment quality through both automatic metrics and human evaluation. They show that models aligned using cross-lingual reward models are preferred by human evaluators over unaligned baselines in over 70% of cases, and surprisingly find that using a reward model from a different language sometimes produces better alignment than using a same-language model. The paper also provides practical guidance for alignment scenarios where language-specific supervised finetuning data is unavailable.
connects: This work builds on the established paradigm of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and preference-based alignment for language models, extending it to the multilingual setting where preference annotations are scarce. It responds to the practical challenge that while alignment techniques like those used in InstructGPT and subsequent work have proven effective for English, obtaining comparable human preference data across diverse languages remains prohibitively expensive. The paper connects to prior work on cross-lingual transfer learning and zero-shot adaptation, applying these principles to the reward modeling component of alignment rather than directly to the language model itself. It also relates to recent research examining the universality of human preferences and whether alignment properties can transfer across languages.
enables: This approach opens pathways for efficiently aligning language models in low-resource and underrepresented languages without requiring extensive multilingual preference annotations. The finding that cross-lingual reward models sometimes outperform same-language models suggests deeper questions about the structure of human preferences and their language-invariance, which could inform future research into universal alignment objectives. The work enables development of better practices for multilingual alignment pipelines, supporting broader deployment of aligned systems across linguistic communities. It points toward investigating whether reward model transfer might work similarly across other dimensions of distribution shift, and suggests opportunities to study how linguistic properties affect the transferability of preference signals and alignment quality.
